column3: Any new VM request which would exceed the hard, per-VM maxima - `chelsea_vm_max_vcpu_count`, `chelsea_vm_max_memory_mib`, and `chelsea_vm_max_volume_mib` - will be rejected, regardless of whether the host could theoretically run it or not.

On startup, unless overprovisioning is enabled (see [Overprovisioning](#overprovisioning)), if either of `chelsea_vm_total_vcpu_count` or `chelsea_vm_total_memory_mib` exceeds the host's vCPU count or memory count respectively, then an error will be returned.

Similarly, on startup, unless overprovisioning is enabled, a warning will be printed if either `chelsea_vm_total_vcpu_count` or `chelsea_vm_total_memory_mib` would leave the host with less than 4 vCPUs or 8192 MiB of memory for non-VM processes.

On receiving a new VM request, Chelsea will first ensure that the new VM would not cause the total amount of "reserved" resources to exceed `chelsea_vm_total_vcpu_count` or `chelsea_vm_total_memory_mib`.

When `chelsea_allow_vcpu_overprovisioning=true` is set, chelsea will not validate the value of `chelsea_vm_total_vcpu_count` against the host vCPU count on start, and similarly when `chelsea_allow_memory_overprovisioning=true` is set, chelsea will not validate the value of `chelsea_vm_total_memory_mib` against the host memory count on start.

When `chelsea_allow_vcpu_overprovisioning=true` and `chelsea_vm_total_vcpu_count=0` are both set, then the value of `chelsea_vm_total_vcpu_count` will be instead treated as infinity (or a very large number) for the purposes of the on-VM-creation reserved resource check and telemetry; the same is true of `chelsea_allow_memory_overprovisioning=true` and `chelsea_vm_total_memory_mib=0`.
On startup, and periodically thereafter, Mulberry will check for issues that may exist on the host.
When a ghost VM is detected by Mulberry, it will not be marked for restart until it has been detected as a ghost VM in `mulberry_ghost_vm_fail_count_restart_threshold` *consecutive* task iterations.
When a ghost VM is marked for restart by Mulberry, an attempt to reboot the VM will be made first.
In the event a ghost VM's reboot fails, then the VM will be fully deleted, with stop code `GhostRebootFailed`.

When Mulberry detects that CPU usage has exceeded `mulberry_cpu_usage_warning_threshold`, or that memory usage (*including* swap usage) has exceeded `mulberry_memory_mib_usage_warning_threshold`, a notification will be logged at warning level.
When a VM in the Sleeping state is Killed, its sleep snapshot must be deleted.
It is only valid to put a VM to Sleep if it is in the 'Paused' or 'Running' state.

The external API may allow a 'Booting' VM to be put to Sleep, if and only if the `skip_wait_boot` param (default false) is false, in which case it will wait for the VM to enter the 'Booting' state before proceeding with the operation as normal.
After successfully putting a VM to Sleep, it will be in the 'Sleeping' state.
If a VM is unsuccessfully put to Sleep, its state will remain unchanged.
When a VM is put to Sleep, a one-time use Firecracker snapshot is created and uploaded to S3.
The Ceph image of a VM that is put to Sleep will not be snapshotted or cloned.
When putting a VM to Sleep, all metadata required to later Wake it will be stored in Postgres, on the `chelsea` schema.
After a snapshot is successfully created for a VM being put to Sleep, the associated process will be killed.
The snapshot associated with a Sleeping VM must never be considered for deletion.
It is only valid to Wake a VM that is in the 'Sleeping' state.
After successfully Waking a VM, it will be in the 'Running' state.
If a Wake fails, the VM will remain in the 'Sleeping' state.
A VM that is Woken must preserve its original ID.
Upon Waking a VM, the snapshot created when it was put to Sleep will be internally marked as used, and associated resources may be considered for freeing.
A VM that is Woken will retain the same VM state (memory, process, etc) that it was put to Sleep with.
A new Ceph image will not be created for a Woken VM; the VM will instead reuse the original image that it was put to Sleep with.
A VM may be Woken on a different host than it was put to Sleep on.
